mappings.rationaleAI: System and state concepts (A.1), first law (A.2), second law (A.3), stable equilibrium and heat (A.4), thermodynamic relations (A.5), third law (A.6), and statistical thermodynamics (A.9).
Nature and properties of gas (B.1), gas laws and equations of state (B.2), and particle-description approach (B.3).
Behavior and properties at equilibrium (C.1), molecular structure (C.2), and transport, acoustic, electrical, thermal, optical properties (C.3).
Basic plasma properties (F.1), collisions (F.2), formation (F.4), behavior in fields (F.5), applications (F.8), and existence in nature (F.9).
Gas (B), liquid (C), plasma (F), and extreme conditions producing exotic states (G).
Temperature governing energy flow (A.4.b), absolute zero and third law (A.6), and matter at extreme temperatures (G).
First law as energy conservation (A.2) and heat in relation to entropy, work, and energy change (A.4.c.v).
Water as a liquid with distinctive properties (C) and central to solutions and solubility (D).
Thermodynamics (A), solutions and solubility (D), and surface phenomena (E).
Thermodynamic cycles related to Carnot cycle (A.5.a), low temperature properties (G.1), and adiabatic cooling methods (G.1.c).
Plasma behavior in electromagnetic fields (F.5).
Maxwell's thermodynamic equations (A.5.b) and Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution for statistical thermodynamics (A.9).
Radiation from plasmas including X rays and synchrotron radiation (F.3).
Earth's atmosphere as a gaseous mixture (B).
Natural gas as a real gas mixture connects to equations of state (B.2).
Lightning as a partially ionized plasma on Earth (F.9).
Basic plasma properties (F.1), collisions (F.2), formation (F.4), behavior in fields (F.5), applications (F.8), and existence in nature (F.9).
Stars as plasmas in nature in the Sun and stars (F.9).

Bose-Einstein statistics contributed to statistical thermodynamics (A.9).
Effects of low temperature on magnetic properties (G.1.a).

CO2 under supercritical conditions connects to equations of state for real gases (B.2.c).
Heat engines relate to the Carnot cycle (A.5.a).
